content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 3,
1994
Robert E.
Leidigh
Law Offices of Olson,
Hagel,
Fong, Leidigh, Waters
& Fishburn
300 Capitol Mall
Suite 350
Sacramento,
CA 95814
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-93—409
Dear Mr. Leidigh:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning a former elected officer’s responsibilities
under the personal use provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”).
1
Because your question is general in nature,
we are
treating your letter as a request for informal assistance pursuant
to Regulation 18329(c) (3) 2
This letter confirms that your letter dated October 20,
1993,
accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you on
that date.
You stated that a former state senator
3 had carried
legislation that was going to be on the November 2,
1993,
special
election ballot.
This former state senator had also established a
ballot measure committee to support that ballot measure.
In the
Haddad Advice Letter,
No.
1-91-132, the Commission stated that a
1
Government Code Sections
81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3) .)
In your letter,
you state that the former elected officer on
whose behalf you are requesting this advice is former State
Senator Barry Keene.
You do not identify the name of the ballot
measure committee controlled by former State Senator Keene to
which he wishes to donate his surplus funds; however,
you did
indicate that the purpose of his ballot measure committee was to
support the passage bf Proposition 169
on the November 2,
1993,
special election ballot.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-93-409
Page 2
candidate could contribute funds to his or her own candidate—
controlled ballot measure committee before the funds became
surplus.
However, that advice letter did not address the question
with respect to funds which had become surplus.
Your inquiry,
therefore,
was whether a former state senator may use his surplus
funds to make a contribution to a ballot measure committee which
he established and now controls.
As I stated in our telephone conversation, pursuant to
Section 89519,
a former elected officer may use surplus campaign
funds to make contributions to support or oppose any ballot
measure or committee,
so long as the ballot, measure committee does
not use the funds to make contributions in support of or
opposition to a candidate for elective office.
(Section 89519(d)
and (e).)
Furthermore,
because the former elected officer was
controlling the ballot measure committee,
I informed you that
another requirement must be met,
namely, that no substantial part
of the contributions to the former elected officer’s ballot
measure committee could have a material financial effect on him,
a
member of his immediate family,
or his campaign treasurer.
4
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-590l.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Deanne Stone
Senior Commission Counsel
For a discussion of what constitutes a “material financial
effect” in the context of permissible uses of campaign funds,
see
the Weiss Advice Letter,
No.
A—91-158.
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

sections.question: Whether a former state senator may use his surplus campaign funds to make a contribution to a ballot measure committee which he established and now controls.
sections.conclusion: Pursuant to Section 89519, a former elected officer may use surplus campaign funds to make contributions to support or oppose any ballot measure or committee, so long as the ballot measure committee does not use the funds to make contributions in support of or opposition to a candidate for elective office. Additionally, no substantial part of the contributions to the former elected officer's ballot measure committee could have a material financial effect on him, a member of his immediate family, or his campaign treasurer.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a former elected officer donate surplus campaign funds to a ballot measure committee that the officer controls?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A former elected officer may contribute surplus campaign funds to a ballot measure committee they control, provided the committee does not use funds for candidate support/opposition and the contributions do not have a material financial effect on the officer, their immediate family, or campaign treasurer.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an informal advice letter confirming previously provided telephone advice. Letter dated February 3, 1994, regarding October 20, 1993 inquiry. No formal opinion immunity provided as this is informal assistance under Regulation 18329(c)(3).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a former state senator may use his surplus campaign funds to make a contribution to a ballot measure committee which he established and now controls.

CONCLUSION: Pursuant to Section 89519, a former elected officer may use surplus campaign funds to make contributions to support or oppose any ballot measure or committee, so long as the ballot measure committee does not use the funds to make contributions in support of or opposition to a candidate for elective office. Additionally, no substantial part of the contributions to the former elected officer's ballot measure committee could have a material financial effect on him, a member of his immediate family, or his campaign treasurer.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms telephone advice regarding former State Senator Barry Keene's ability to donate surplus campaign funds to a ballot measure committee supporting Proposition 169 that he controls, subject to restrictions under Section 89519.
